serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving consists of four grilled oysters topped with cheese, weighing approximately 200 grams.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central (various entries for components)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are estimated based on a weighted average of primary components: oysters (raw, Pacific, wild, FDC ID: 170451), mozzarella cheese (part-skim, FDC ID: 170889), and butter (salted, FDC ID: 170879). Extended micronutrient values (monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, vitamins, minerals) are not consistently reported across all three component entries in USDA FoodData Central for this composite estimation and are therefore set to -1.

gi_source.title: University of Sydney GI Database (Oysters, Cheddar Cheese)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Glycemic index is estimated as low (set to 1) because both oysters and cheese are very low in carbohydrates and have a negligible impact on blood glucose. Glycemic load is 0.0 as the carbohydrate content per serving is very low.

hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should consume grilled oysters with cheese in moderation due to its sodium content of approximately 316 mg per 100 g, and should opt for reduced-sodium cheese options where available.

type2_diabetes.guidance: Grilled oysters with cheese can be a suitable option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low carbohydrate content (approximately 3.9 g per 100 g) and negligible glycemic index, though portion control remains important for overall calorie and saturated fat intake.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Those with cardiovascular disease should consume grilled oysters with cheese sparingly, as the saturated fat content (approximately 9 g per 100 g) from cheese and butter may adversely impact LDL cholesterol levels.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grilled Oysters with Cheese, known in Vietnamese as 'Hàu nướng phô mai', is a popular and savory appetizer or snack in Vietnamese cuisine. This dish typically features fresh oysters, often large and plump, grilled or baked until tender. They are generously topped with a rich, creamy mixture of cheese, often a blend of mozzarella and Parmesan, sometimes combined with butter, garlic, and a hint of herbs. The cheese topping is melted and lightly browned, creating a delightful contrast with the briny, succulent oyster meat. Nutritionally, this dish is a good source of protein (approximately 11.6 g per 100 g) and contributes significant fat (approximately 15.5 g per 100 g), predominantly saturated fat from the cheese and butter. Its carbohydrate content is low at approximately 3.9 g per 100 g, resulting in a negligible glycemic index and making it broadly suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. Sodium content is moderate at approximately 316 mg per 100 g. Individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease should be mindful of portion sizes due to the fat and sodium content.
